Title: A natural antisense transcript of the Petunia hybrida Sho gene suggests a role for an antisense mechanism in cytokinin regulation

Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The Sho locus contains an antisense-specific promoter that produces a partially overlapping natural antisense transcript (NAT) opposite to Sho. 2. Sho AS expression generally tracks Sho sense transcript levels across tissues, but its promoter is locally enhanced in newly formed or reorganized tissues induced by prolonged cytokinin treatment and in nodal tissues under apical dominance. 3. The overlapping Sho/Sho AS region gives rise to dsRNA and Sho-specific small RNAs, supporting a role for Sho AS in reducing local Sho transcript abundance and thereby adjusting local cytokinin synthesis.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in most tested tissues; relatively high in flower buds and nodal stem tissue; promoter activity enhanced in cytokinin-induced callus-like tissues and reduced in upper nodes after decapitation-induced axillary shoot outgrowth.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant hormone controlling growth and development; also involved in carbon transport and metabolism.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Non-coding natural antisense transcript; antisense ORF showed no match to known proteins.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in multiple tissues with highest small-RNA-associated representation in flower buds; promoter active in nodes and flower buds.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The antisense ORF itself has no known protein match, and its regulatory effect is inferred through RNA overlap, dsRNA formation, and small RNA production rather than protein function. Type I (~35 nt) antisense-specific RNA is found in all tissues, whereas type II (~24 nt) sense/antisense RNAs are absent from roots, suggesting tissue-specific modulation of NAT-mediated degradation.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tool to modulate local cytokinin biosynthesis via antisense-based regulation rather than coding-sequence editing; may enable tissue-specific tuning of cytokinin levels, though practical breeding utility is not directly tested.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes antisense-mediated downregulation of Sho, likely decreasing local cytokinin synthesis in specific tissues.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Prolonged cytokinin treatment (BAP, iPA, zeatin riboside) causing dedifferentiation/callus formation; decapitation affecting nodal tissue state; GA3 and IAA showed no comparable effect on promoter pattern in the assay.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots are described as the main source of cytokinin supply/synthesis.
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native Sho antisense promoter PR2; endogenous/tissue-responsive promoter. Reporter assays also used PR2-GUS.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Sho; putative DICER activity; putative RdRP complex
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Antisense-promoter GUS reporter assays; strand-specific RT-PCR; dsRNA detection after RNaseONE treatment; small RNA northern blotting; small RNA cloning/sequencing; terminator exonuclease assay
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential reduction of cytokinin synthesis in specific tissues could alter growth patterns or organ development, but no direct fitness or yield penalty was tested.

Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Sho encodes an isopentenyltransferase responsible for cytokinin synthesis in Petunia hybrida. 2. The Sho locus produces both sense and overlapping antisense transcripts, and evidence of dsRNA plus Sho-specific small RNAs suggests that Sho transcript abundance is post-transcriptionally modulated by an antisense mechanism. 3. Tissue-specific activation of the antisense system is proposed to adjust local cytokinin synthesis, with roots maintaining cytokinin production by lacking the ~24 nt Sho small-RNA pool.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native Sho sense promoter PR1; endogenous promoter. Reporter assays also used PR1-GUS.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes regulation of Sho transcript turnover through its natural antisense transcript rather than direct genetic manipulation of Sho coding sequence. Sho is described as the only known IPT-encoding gene in Petunia hybrida in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an isopentenyl group to adenine nucleotides (ATP/ADP) in the rate-limiting step of cytokinin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Sho sense transcript detected across multiple tissues; relatively increased versus antisense in roots and young leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Rate-limiting step in cytokinin synthesis, as stated for IPT genes and Sho being an IPT-encoding gene.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies committed/rate-limiting step toward cytokinin production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes cytokinin biosynthesis; its local downregulation via antisense-associated RNA degradation is proposed to decrease local cytokinin levels.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering cytokinin levels in a tissue-specific manner, potentially through promoter or antisense-module manipulation instead of complete loss-of-function; caution warranted because cytokinin broadly affects development.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Sho AS; putative DICER activity; putative RdRP complex
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots are the main source of cytokinin synthesis/supply.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbation of Sho likely alters plant growth and developmental processes governed by cytokinin; direct tradeoffs were not experimentally quantified.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prior gene identity as cytokinin-synthesis enzyme referenced in this study; strand-specific RT-PCR; antisense-promoter/sense-promoter reporter assays; dsRNA and small RNA detection linked to Sho locus
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential hormones for plant growth and development; also involved in carbon transport and metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in most tissues tested; relatively higher than antisense in roots and young leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Prolonged cytokinin exposure altered antisense promoter activity in dedifferentiated tissues; decapitation changed nodal antisense promoter activity and is relevant to local Sho regulation.
